coredata.dc:title: Green construction of metal- and additive-free citrus peel-derived carbon dot/g-C3N4 photocatalysts for the high-performance photocatalytic decomposition of sunset yellow
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Dezhuang
Guo, Jialiang
Wang, Fan
Zhang, Jing
Song, Shuang
Feng, Bingxin
Zhang, Xiuling
Zhang, Wentao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, novel, metal-free, CP-derived CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (CDCNs) were created by introducing citrus peel-derived carbon dots (CP-derived CDs) into graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) by a green hydrothermal method. The CDCNs were revealed to have superior photoelectrochemical properties relative to pristine g-C3N4 for the photocatalytic degradation of the food dye sunset yellow (SY) under visible light. For SY decomposition, the recommended catalyst contributed almost 96.3% to the photodegradation rate after 60 min of irradiation, showing satisfactory reusability, structural stability and biocompatibility. Moreover, a mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic SY degradation was proposed according to band analysis, free radical trapping and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) results. A possible pathway for SY photodegradation was also predicted from UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) results. The constructed nonmetallic nanophotocatalysts afford a novel route for the elimination of harmful dyes and for the resource conversion of citrus peels.

head.ce:title: Green construction of metal- and additive-free citrus peel-derived carbon dot/g-C3N4 photocatalysts for the high-performance photocatalytic decomposition of sunset yellow

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Changchun Normal University, Changchun, Jilin 130032, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Changchun Normal University, Changchun, Jilin 130032, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province 150030, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, novel, metal-free, CP-derived CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (CDCNs) were created by introducing citrus peel-derived carbon dots (CP-derived CDs) into graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) by a green hydrothermal method. The CDCNs were revealed to have superior photoelectrochemical properties relative to pristine g-C3N4 for the photocatalytic degradation of the food dye sunset yellow (SY) under visible light. For SY decomposition, the recommended catalyst contributed almost 96.3% to the photodegradation rate after 60 min of irradiation, showing satisfactory reusability, structural stability and biocompatibility. Moreover, a mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic SY degradation was proposed according to band analysis, free radical trapping and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) results. A possible pathway for SY photodegradation was also predicted from UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) results. The constructed nonmetallic nanophotocatalysts afford a novel route for the elimination of harmful dyes and for the resource conversion of citrus peels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Sunset Yellow (SY), also known as Food Yellow 3, is a water-soluble yellow mono-azo dye. As an artificial food colorant, SY has been intensively applied in the fields of food and beverage processing because of its strong tinting ability, good color fastness and low production cost (Calam & Çakıcı, 2023). However, due to the interaction between hemoglobin/human serum albumin and SY, excessive consumption of SY has harmful side effects on human health and may lead to serious health hazards such as renal failure, hepatocellular damage, and cancer (Sun et al., 2023). For this reason, developing a new, simple and reliable degradation method to efficiently remove SY is imperative for human health.

Lately, biological and photocatalytic techniques have shown advantages such as high efficiency, low cost and eco-friendliness and are believed to be adaptive green methods for the elimination of toxic dyes (Mehrizad, & Gharbani, 2017; Barathi, Aruljothi, Karthik, Padikasan, & Ashokkumar, 2022; Duan et al., 2020). As a nonmetallic semiconductor photocatalytic material, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has received significant attention for the photocatalytic decontamination of harmful pollutants owing to its availability, inherent optical properties, tunable electronic structure and nontoxicity (Fard, Khojasteh, & Gharbani, 2018). Under light irradiation, molecular oxygen can be effectively activated on the surface of g-C3N4 nanomaterials to initiate radical-mediated photocatalytic reactions. Nonetheless, insufficient visible-light absorption and rapid photocarrier recombination limit the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 (Zhang et al., 2022). To overcome such drawbacks, various promising strategies have been explored to enhance the photoreactivity of g-C3N4, including element doping, morphology control and the construction of heterojunctions (AttariKhasraghi, Zare, Mehrizad, Modirshahla, & Behnajady, 2021). Although the above modification strategies have been indicated to improve photocatalytic activity to a certain extent, potentially harmful precious metals are typically applied.

As a nonmetallic carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been used as an initiator to improve the photoactivity of g-C3N4 owing to their special optical features (Jian et al., 2016). Many studies have demonstrated that stronger visible-light absorption can be obtained by modifying g-C3N4 with nonmetallic CDs (Wang et al., 2018; Duan et al., 2020). However, conventional compounding approaches usually involve the introduction of additional chemicals as passivators or cocatalysts, which may cause serious ecological hazards and resource wastage (Zhang et al., 2022). Coupling g-C3N4 with CDs prepared from natural biological waste resources is considered to be a possible choice in this circumstance. Citrus peels (CPs) are nontoxic biowastes obtained from natural sources and contain a complex variety of metabolic components (mainly composed of carbon) (Markovic et al., 2023). They can be used as carbon sources to prepare element-self-doped CDs with capture properties without the addition of passivators and doping components (Gudimella et al., 2021). As a result, CPs are ideal candidates for fabricating metal- and additive-free carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. The effective utilization of citrus peel waste is also conducive to reducing the wastage of resources.

In this work, citrus peel waste was employed as a bioprecursor to produce carbon dots with self-doped elements via a green and straightforward hydrothermal method. Novel metal-free CP-derived CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (CDCNs) were then obtained by the hydrothermal self-assembly of CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4. Various characterizations were conducted to clarify the morphology, structure and photoelectrochemical properties of the synthesized samples. Afterward, an efficient, economical and environmentally friendly photodecomposition approach based on the reinforced photoactivity of CDCNs was developed and applied to the high-efficiency removal of the food dye SY. Moreover, the underlying mechanism of CDCNs for SY photodecomposition is fully discussed, and the biosafety of CDCNs is verified. The as-synthesized CDCNs show great potential for hazardous dye elimination, biocatalysis and sustainable development.

CP was collected from a local fruit supermarket in Harbin, China. Urea and sunset yellow were supplied by Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Isopropanol (IPA) was purchased from Dahe Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade p-benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3) and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were procured from Sinopharm Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of the CP-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Citrus peel-derived carbon dots (CP-derived CDs) were prepared using a simple one-step green hydrothermal treatment (Gogoi, Koyani, Golder, & Peela, 2020). Briefly, citrus peel waste was washed and dried and then triturated after cooling. 1 g of dried CP powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 35 mL of deionized (DI) water and stirred for 20 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then, the resulting mixture solution was sealed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave with a 100 mL capacity and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the brown aqueous solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to acquire CP-derived CDs. The as-obtained CP-derived CDs were then freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C. The yield of CP-derived CDs was approximately 10% (0.1 g per g of dry citrus peel powder).

The g-C3N4 was manufactured using urea as the precursor based on a reported method (Liu et al., 2022). Generally, 20 g of ground urea was placed into a clean ceramic crucible with a cover followed by heating to 550 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace (ramp-up rate: 15 °C/min). After the material was calcined and cooled to room temperature, the obtained earthy yellow g-C3N4 was granulated to powder for further use.

CDCNs decorated with various mass percentages of CP-derived CDs were fabricated following a facile hydrothermal self-assembly strategy. First, g-C3N4 (500 mg) and the required quantity of CP-derived CDs (25 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg and 150 mg) were suspended in 50 mL of DI water before being adequately stirred and sonicated for 2 h to produce a brown mixed solution with brownish-yellow particles. Second, the reaction suspension was carefully moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 4 h, resulting in the target CDCNs. The resultant photocatalysts were labeled CDCN-x, where “x” represents the mass percentage of the CP-derived CDs used to produce the g-C3N4 (x = 5%, 10%, 20%, and 30%). As a comparative photocatalyst, a conventional CDs/g-C3N4 (named CCN) control sample was fabricated by directly mixing 500 mg g-C3N4 with 100 mg CD freeze-dried powder that was prepared with citric acid as the sole carbon source.

The phase composition of the fabricated products was surveyed using an X‘Pert Pro MPD diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, USA). The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU-8010, Tokyo, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Kyoto, Japan). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) were collected using a UV-3600i Plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), and UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by a UV-2700i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on an RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). A CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, Shanghai, China) was used to investigate the photoelectrochemical properties of the photocatalysts. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was conducted on an SPD-M20A HPLC instrument (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). All photocatalytic degradation measurements were carried out under visible light by employing a 300 W Xenon lamp (Beijing Perfectlight, Beijing, China) with an ultraviolet cutoff and an infrared filter (420 < λ < 780 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation measurements of the food dye SY
ce:section.ce:para: In each experiment on SY photodegradation, 5 mL of the synthesized catalyst aqueous dispersion was added to 45 mL of SY solution (2.5 mg/L). The resulting mixture was slowly stirred for 30 min under dark conditions to establish absorption–desorption equilibrium of the sample and SY before it was exposed to visible light for SY photodegradation. As the reaction proceeded, 3 mL aliquots of the above-mixed solution were sampled at 15 min intervals, and the suspended particles were removed by filtration with a Millipore filter (0.22 μm). The peak absorbance of the filtrate at 482 nm was recorded using a UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrometer to analyze the residual SY concentration. In addition, four consecutive cycle tests were conducted to explore the reusability and photochemical stability of the CDCNs. The SY photodegradation rate was assessed by the equation C = Ct/Ci × 100%, in which C corresponds to the relative concentration of SY before and after photocatalytic decomposition and Ct and Ci represent the initial concentration of SY and the remaining concentration of SY after a specified time interval (t), respectively. The final products of SY photodecomposition were analyzed with UV–vis spectroscopy and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The biological safety of the prepared nanocomposite photocatalyst was systematically assayed by cytocompatibility and hemolysis tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism exploration of SY photodegradation by CDCNs
ce:section.ce:para:
Radical-trapping tests were conducted using p-benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3), isopropanol (IPA) and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), which serve as superoxide iron radical (O2
−), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and hole (h+) scavengers, respectively. Following the former photodecomposition experimental procedure, the four quenchers were individually introduced into the SY photodegradation system to ascertain the role of various reactive species. Furthermore, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) patterns of the reactive species were directly obtained on a Bruker EMX Plus spectrometer. O2
− and
OH were detected using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the electron spin-trapping agent. 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP) was applied for the detection of 1O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CP-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots with exceptional water solubility and optical properties were synthesized from citrus peel waste via a simple one-step hydrothermal process. Citrus peel contains abundant structural cellulose, hemicellulose and crude proteins (Gudimella et al., 2021). These primary components were hydrolyzed into amino acids and glucose and then conjugated with other soluble sugars, ascorbic acid and phenols. Subsequently, these low molecular weight organic compounds were decomposed and converted into CD with heteroatom doping and passivation involving a “bottom-up” approach (Olmos-Moya, Velazquez-Martinez, Pineda-Arellano, Rangel-Mendez, & Chazaro-Ruiz, 2022). The structural characteristics of the as-synthesized CP-derived CDs were investigated by FTIR analysis. In Fig. 1 a, the broad bands at 3270–3560 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups, and the peak at 2937 cm−1 is in accord with the stretching vibrations of CH (Ramar, Moothattu, & Balasubramanian, 2018). These characteristic bands demonstrate the presence of a host of phenolic-hydroxyl, carboxyl-hydroxyl, and amine functional groups on the surface of the prepared CP-derived CDs, resulting in the excellent hydrophilic properties of the CDs (Hu, Li, Qiu, & Sun, 2019). The absorption peaks at 1702 cm−1, 1126 cm−1 and 1091 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of CO, C-NH-C and CO bonds, respectively (Li et al., 2022). The peak at 1611 cm−1 is due to the CC skeleton stretching vibration in the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structure of CP-derived CDs (Ramezani, Qorbanpour, & Rahbar, 2018). Furthermore, the peak at 1396 cm−1 corresponding to the C-NH2 stretching vibration of primary amines demonstrates the existence of amino functional groups on the CP-derived CD surface (Olmos-Moya, Velazquez-Martinez, Pineda-Arellano, Rangel-Mendez, & Chazaro-Ruiz, 2022). The surface elemental compositions of CP-derived CDs were further characterized by XPS measurement (Fig. 1b). The four peaks at 285.0, 346.9, 399.9 and 532.0 eV correspond to C 1s, Ca 2p, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, clearly suggesting that the as-prepared CP-derived CDs are self-doped with N and Ca in addition to C and O (Sharma et al., 2023).

The distinctive optical properties of the as-prepared CP-derived CDs were analyzed through UV–vis absorption spectra, optical photographs and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1c, the UV–Vis absorption of the CP-derived CDs exhibits three intrinsic peaks at 241 nm, 279 nm and 348 nm. The broad band at 241 nm is assigned to π-π* electron transitions of the carbon core sp2 aromatic structure (CC conjugate system) of CP-derived CDs and is also related to the doping of N atoms containing abundant electrons (Kim & Jo, 2020). The absorption band at 279 nm is indicative of the n-π* edge transitions of the surface functional groups. Another band at 348 nm may be related to the redshifted surface state of co-functionalized CP-derived CDs, which is caused by the n-π* electron transitions of the CO and C-NH2 moieties on CP-derived CDs (Olmos-Moya, Velazquez-Martinez, Pineda-Arellano, Rangel-Mendez, & Chazaro-Ruiz, 2022). Subsequently, the as-prepared CP-derived CDs present bright and strong blue fluorescence emission behavior at 482 nm under optimal excitation at near 390 nm (Fig. 1c). More intuitively, the optical photographs in the inset of Fig. 1c show that the CP-derived CDs form a yellowish transparent liquid under daylight and emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The blue fluorescence of CP-derived CDs is further confirmed through the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) chromatic coordinates at (0.2160, 0.3028), which are obtained from the fluorescence emission spectra excited at 390 nm (Fig. 1d). In addition, the CP-derived CDs exhibit representative excitation-dependent photoluminescence features in Fig. 1e due to the unique surface molecular states created by various functional groups. As the excitation wavelength was changed from 300 to 500 nm, the fluorescence emission peak of CP-derived CDs exhibited a redshift, and the emission peak intensity first strengthened and subsequently weakened. Similarly, the 3D fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 1f clearly shows that the emission peak position changes with increasing excitation wavelength, which further reveals the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CP-derived CDs related to photon reabsorption. All the above results suggest the successful fabrication of CP-derived CDs with amorphous carbon structures and unique optical properties.

The CDCNs were fabricated with g-C3N4 and CP-derived CDs by a green two-step hydrothermal method. The crystalline properties of pristine g-C3N4 and the synthesized CDCNs loaded with various percentages of CP-derived CDs were analyzed by XRD patterns, as exhibited in Fig. S1a. In pristine g-C3N4, the two original peaks at approximately 13.1° and 27.6° are related to the (100) in-plane packing of heptazine units and interlayer stacking of the graphite-like crystal structure in (002), respectively (JCPDS No. 87-1526) (Khan, Han, Khan, Karim, & Cho, 2018). When CP-derived CDs were incorporated, both the (100) and (002) phases were retained with reduced intensity because CP-derived CDs hinder interlayer stacking and in-plane packing in g-C3N4 (Si et al., 2021). Noticeably, compared with pure g-C3N4, the (002) diffraction peak of CDCNs shifted from 27.60° to 27.45° (inset in Fig. S1a), implying that the interlayer spacing of the graphite-like plane increased. This might be attributed to the successful coupling of CP-derived CDs with g-C3N4, resulting in C and N bond cleavage or abundant oxygen addition from CP-derived CDs. In addition, no additional impurity peaks were observed during the synthesis process, which revealed that a high-purity crystalline state remained in the synthesized CDCNs.

FTIR spectra of the bare g-C3N4 and as-prepared CDCNs with different CP-derived CD loading amounts were recorded to further explore the structural composition of the as-obtained samples (Fig. S1b). All samples displayed similar typical absorption peaks centered at 808 cm−1, 1100–1700 cm−1, and 2950–3350 cm−1, which corresponded to the respiratory mode of the s-triazine units, stretching vibration modes of CN/CN heterocycles, and residual NH/OH stretching vibrations, respectively (Duan et al., 2020). This phenomenon suggested that the skeleton structure of g-C3N4 was well maintained in the synthesized CDCNs with the implantation of CP-derived CDs. Interestingly, compared with pristine g-C3N4, the CDCNs displayed a sharp absorption peak at 1075 cm−1, revealing that polar oxygen-containing functional groups (COC stretching) might exist under CP-derived CD doping (Ma et al., 2017). In addition, the characteristic signal of CC tensile at 1648 cm−1 was detected in CDCNs, which could be attributed to the photoluminescence caused by the π-π* transition of CP-derived CDs (Qin & Zeng, 2017). As mentioned above, the XRD and FTIR results revealed that interactions between as-formed functional groups were initiated between CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4.

To understand the interactions in more detail, XPS characterization was carried out to further probe the specific chemical structures as well as elemental compositions of the resultant CDCN-20%. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, both g-C3N4 and typical CDCN-20% show C 1s, N 1s and O 1s signals in the full survey spectra. More importantly, the characteristic signals of C 1s and O 1s in CDCN-20% were significantly stronger than those of pure g-C3N4, which again verified the successful loading of CP-derived CDs. Furthermore, the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s spectra of pure g-C3N4 and typical CDCN-20% were deconvoluted, as shown in Fig. 2(b-d). Specifically, the C 1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2b) of g-C3N4 deconvoluted into three peaks at binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.3 eV, attributed to graphitic CC/CC, CO and aromatic sp2-bonded carbon (N-CN), respectively (Jian et al., 2016). As CP-derived CDs were added to g-C3N4, the CO peak increased and shifted to 286.5 eV, which might be attributed to the formation of COC bonds between g-C3N4 and CP-derived CDs through the mutual bridging of surface –OH groups (Li et al., 2019). The N 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of CDCN-20% shown in Fig. 2c was separated into four peaks with binding energies of 398.6, 400.1, 401.1 eV and 404.8, which represented sp2-N (C-NC) in the triazine rings, tertiary N (N-(C)3), NH in the –NH2 group, and the charging effects of the heterocycles, respectively (Zhang et al., 2019). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2d, in addition to the OH peak (532.6 eV), also showed two new peaks at 532.0 eV and 533.4 eV that did not appear in spectrum of bare g-C3N4. The peak located at 532.0 eV corresponds to the epoxide structure (CO), while the O component located at 533.4 eV is assigned to COC/C-OH (Zhao et al., 2022). Consequently, the above XPS analysis reasonably confirmed the successful combination of CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 through strong interactions, which was consistent with the results of FTIR and XRD.

The morphology and microstructure of the synthesized CP-derived CDs, g-C3N4 and representative CDCN-20% were investigated by SEM, TEM and high-resolution TEM. The SEM image of Fig. S2a depicts a crinkly layered nanosheet structure for g-C3N4; however, the formation of an irregular rough coral-like nanosheet structure covered by small spherical nanoparticles was observed for CDCN-20% (Fig. S2b). Fig. S2(c-d) displays the TEM and high-magnification TEM images of the as-prepared CP-derived CDs, respectively. The CP-derived CDs appeared as quasispherical monodisperse nanoparticles with mean diameters of ∼5 nm. Furthermore, TEM images of the prepared pure g-C3N4 and representative CDCN-20% are presented in Fig. S2(e-f) to indicate the existence of CP-derived CDs. Pristine g-C3N4 exhibits a typical laminated nanosheet structure and smooth surface (Fig. S2e). The TEM image of the CDCN-20% sample in Fig. S2f shows that CP-derived CDs can be found on the g-C3N4 nanosheets, with the red circles and blue arrows representing the CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets, respectively. These SEM and TEM results provide reliable evidence for the successful combination of CP-derived CDs with g-C3N4 nanosheets.

The photogenerated charge recombination process of the as-synthesized photocatalysts was investigated by PL spectra (Fig. 3b). Pure g-C3N4 exhibited an intense intrinsic fluorescence emission band at 440 nm (excited at 370 nm), relating to the rapid band-to-band recombination of the photogeneration carriers. Normally, the fluorescence emission intensity is negatively correlated with the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers (Qin & Zeng, 2017). After the incorporation of CP-derived CDs, the PL intensity of the CDCNs decreased dramatically, signifying that the radiative recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs was effectively suppressed by CP-derived CD modification. In particular, CDCN-20% displayed the lowest PL emission peak, which implied that it possessed the most efficient separation and transfer of photoexcited charge carriers; this was consistent with the UV–vis DRS results. Otherwise, a slight redshift of CDCNs was observed in PL, which was related to the reductive band gap width of the composites following decoration with CP-derived CDs (Wang et al., 2018).

To further elucidate the separation and transfer behaviors of photoinduced charge visually, the photoelectrochemical properties of the original g-C3N4 and representative CDCN-20% were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and transient photocurrent (TPC) response. Fig. 3c depicts the EIS Nyquist plots of pristine g-C3N4 and CDCN-20%. A positive correlation between the arc radius of the Nyquist plots and the charge transfer resistance is well known (Wu et al., 2018). The impedance arc radius of CDCN-20% was much smaller than that of pristine g-C3N4, which demonstrated that a larger electrical conductivity could be obtained after the combining of g-C3N4 and CP-derived CDs; hence, preeminent charge separation and transport performance were achieved. In addition, the TPC spectra (Fig. 3d) show that the photocurrent density of CDCN-20% was distinctly increased compared with that of pure g-C3N4 with multiple periodic on/off visible-light irradiation, further confirming the greatly enhanced photogenerated charge separation and migration efficiency. The EIS results were consistent with the TPC response analysis. Therefore, based on the PL, EIS, and TPC responses, it can be concluded that an appropriate combination of CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 can efficiently promote the separation and transfer of photoinduced charges, thereby adequately improving photocatalytic activity. This improvement in photoelectrochemical performance can be attributed to the distinctive electron transport properties induced by CP-derived CDs and the tight interfacial connection between CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 (Chen et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the SY photodecomposition efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The as-fabricated samples were investigated as photocatalysts to facilitate the photodegradation removal of the food dye SY under visible light. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the catalytic degradation curve of SY showed the relative concentration variation in SY within 90 min of treatment time, including that occurring during adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the samples and SY molecules for 30 min. The relative concentration change in SY was negligible in the absence of a photocatalyst (visible-light irradiation alone), demonstrating that SY possessed high photochemical stability. In comparison, in the experimental groups with photocatalysts, the relative concentration of SY declined as a function of photocatalytic time. Under identical experimental conditions, the SY removal efficiencies of pristine g-C3N4 and CP-derived CDs were only 48.9% and 0.03% within 60 min, respectively. However, the SY photodegradation efficiencies of CDCN-5%, CDCN-10%, CDCN-20% and CDCN-30% were 73.0%, 82.7%, 96.3% and 88.4%, respectively, which confirmed the elimination of SY and the conspicuously improved photodegradation activity of the CDCNs. In the presence of the CCN composites fabricated via a conventional method, less than 65% of SY was eliminated after 60 min of visible-light irradiation. This was probably due to the poor interaction between g-C3N4 and conventional CDs and the low electron-withdrawing ability of traditional CDs (Chen et al., 2021). In contrast, the obtained CDCNs exhibited considerably enhanced visible-light response due to the photosensitivity and capturing property of the homogeneously dispersed CP-derived CDs, which could markedly improve the separation of photoelectrons and holes. With an increase in CP-derived CD addition from CDCN-5% to CDCN-30%, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency initially increased and then decreased. Compared with all samples, CDCN-20%, which had the optimal CP-derived CD content, showed the strongest photodegradation ability, and SY was almost completely decomposed after 60 min. This may be related to an insufficient increase in active sites when the CP-derived CD loading is low, resulting in lower separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. When the content of CP-derived CDs is high, CP-derived CDs may cover the photoactive sites of CDCNs, leading to a decrease in the degradation performance of the photocatalyst.

In Fig. 4b, the overall photodegradation of SY conformed to a pseudofirst-order reaction, and the corresponding SY degradation kinetics within 60 min were analyzed according to the formula ln (C t/C 0) = −k·t (k, t, C t and C 0 are the apparent rate constant, irradiation time, SY concentration at time t, and initial SY concentration, respectively) (Si et al., 2021). All the CDCNs that were decorated with various percentages of CP-derived CDs exhibited higher reaction rate constants than pristine g-C3N4, CP-derived CDs and CCN, indicating that the CDCNs had higher efficiency for SY photocatalytic degradation than the original g-C3N4, CP-derived CDs and CCN. CDCN-20% demonstrated a maximum reaction rate constant k value of 0.04684 min−1, which was 4.05, 75.55 and 2.54 times that of the original g-C3N4 (0.01157 min−1), CP-derived CDs (0.00062 min−1) and CCN (0.01845 min−1), respectively. This showed that the CDCN-20% photocatalyst obtained by combining g-C3N4 with CP-derived CDs possessed notably enhanced photocatalytic performance. In addition, the reaction rate constant k values were 0.02112, 0.02961 and 0.03390 min−1 for CDCN-5%, CDCN-10% and CDCN-30%, respectively, which were far higher than those of CP-derived CDs and pristine g-C3N4. The above results showed that among the analogs, the as-prepared CDCNs possessed satisfactory visible-light-induced photocatalytic properties, which was principally related to the photosensitivity and the capacity for charge carrier recombination suppression of the CP-derived CDs. The visible absorption of SY for removal times over 90 min was synchronously monitored by UV–Vis spectroscopy in the dark and during the subsequent photodegradation process. According to the results of UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 4c), the intensity of the SY characteristic absorption peak at 482 nm diminished slightly within the first 30 min (adsorption–desorption equilibrium). In the next 60 min, the SY feature peak was strongly quenched and nearly disappeared with the extension of irradiation time, which was consistent with the results of photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 4a). Correspondingly, the color of the SY solution increasingly changed from yellow to colorless, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4c. Over 90 min, the featured absorbance peak of SY continuously weakened but without an evident position shift, suggesting that SY was ultimately decomposed to low molecular weight products. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) was also calculated for SY degradation (Fig. S3) under monochromatic light at 420 nm according to previous reports (Guaraldo, Vakili, Wenk, & Mattia, 2023) to compare the activities of the obtained photocatalysts. The CDCN-20% system showed the highest AQY value for SY degradation, suggesting it absorbed more photons and provided higher photocatalytic activity; this is consistent with the UV–vis DRS results. Additionally, the aqueous suspension obtained before and after the photocatalytic removal of SY by CDCN-20% was further analyzed by HPLC; the results are presented in Fig. 4d. The characteristic chromatographic peak of SY at a retention time of 9.477 min basically disappeared after photocatalytic degradation, and no additional chromatographic peaks appeared. The HPLC results matched well with the visible absorption curves, which revealed that SY was ultimately decomposed to low molecular weight compounds without any byproducts.

To further assess the photocatalytic stability and recyclability of CDCNs for the removal of SY, four cyclic degradation measurements were carried out using the representative CDCN-20% photocatalyst in fresh SY solution under the same experimental conditions. Following each run, the used CDCN-20% was recovered and cleaned thoroughly with DI water before the operations were repeated. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the photocatalytic performance of the CDCN-20% sample marginally decreased after each cycle; however, an SY removal efficiency of 90.9% was still obtained in the fourth run. Additionally, the structural stability of CDCN-20% after four catalytic photodegradation reactions of SY was evaluated by FTIR and TEM. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4f) of the used and fresh CDCN-20% photocatalyst displayed no distinct differences in the peak positions. The morphological properties of CDCN-20% after four SY catalytic photodegradation reactions are shown in Fig. S4. The used CDCN-20% exhibited morphological characteristics similar to those of fresh CDCN-20%. These results highlight the excellent structural stability of the photocatalyst. To assess the biosafety of the obtained nanocomposite photocatalyst, the cytotoxicity and hemocompatibility of typical CDCN-20% were systematically explored. The impact of CDCN-20% on the viability of L929 cells was first measured via the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. S5a, the relative viability of cells treated with various concentrations of CDCN-20% was between 95% and 110%, reflecting that the CDCNs had no significant toxic effect on L929 cells. Next, the hemolysis behavior of CDCN-20% with human red blood cells was explored in vitro. The relative hemolysis ratio remained at a level of 1.47% (far below 5%), even at a 500 μg/mL concentration (Fig. S5b), demonstrating that the developed CDCNs had excellent blood compatibility; this matched with the clear supernatant of all CDCN-20% treatment groups in the inset of Fig. S5b. All the above results indicated that the developed nanocomposite photocatalysts presented excellent reusability, structural stability and biological safety. Taken together, the CDCNs can be employed as a safe, efficient and stable photocatalyst for the degradation of harmful food dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reasons for enhanced SY photodecomposition by the CDCNs
ce:section.ce:para:
To gain insight into the reinforced photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the CDCNs for SY degradation, radical-scavenging tests were performed to identify the active species
OH in IPA, O2
− in BQ, 1O2 in NaN3 and h+ in Na2C2O4 (Fig. 5 a). Clearly, the Na2C2O4 quencher exhibited negligible inhibition of SY removal, hinting that h+ was not the main reactive radical for SY decomposition in the photocatalytic system. In contrast, SY photocatalytic degradation was reduced after the introduction of the quenchers BQ, IPA and NaN3, and the inhibitory effect of BQ was strongest. The SY removal rate decreased to approximately 24.0% in the photocatalytic reaction system after BQ was added. These results signify that O2
− was the most substantial active radical for the catalytic photodecomposition of SY, followed by 1O2 and
OH. The following reactive species generation mechanism for CDCNs during the photocatalytic removal of SY was proposed:

The role of reactive species (O2
−, 1O2 and
OH) during the photocatalytic removal of SY was further verified by an EPR technique using DMPO or TEMP as the appropriate spin probes. As shown in Fig. 5b-d, no EPR response signals of DMPO-O2
−, TEMP-1O2 and DMPO-
OH were found in the presence of the CDCN-20% photocatalyst in the dark. The EPR signals of the reactive species, especially the O2
− produced by the CDCN-20% photocatalyst, were clearly detected under visible-light irradiation, which confirmed the decisive role of O2
− in the enhanced photocatalytic reaction system and was consistent with the results of the radical-trapping tests.

The flat band potential (E fb) values of g-C3N4 and CDCN-20% measured by the X-axis intercept in the Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. S6a) were −0.87 V and −0.66 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively, which corresponded to −0.67 and −0.46 V vs. the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) (Zhang et al., 2023). The positive tangent slopes indicate typical n-type semiconductors. Since the conduction band (CB) potential (E CB) is approximately 0.20 eV lower than the E fb for n-type semiconductors, the E CB of g-C3N4 and CDCN-20% were assessed to be −0.87 and −0.66 eV, respectively (Dang et al., 2023). Subsequently, the valence band (VB) potential (E VB) values of pure g-C3N4 and CDCN-20% could be calculated using the equation E g = E VB − E CB (Ranjith et al., 2023; Vignesh et al., 2021). The E g values of neat g-C3N4 and CDCN-20% were 2.72 and 2.55 eV (the inset of Fig. 3a), respectively, while their corresponding E VB values were +1.85 and +1.89 eV. In Fig. S6b, the band structures formed by CP-derived CDs and the g-C3N4 interface were established according to the E g, E CB and E VB values.

Based on these results, a plausible mechanism for the enhanced SY photodecomposition efficiency of CDCN-20% was proposed and is presented in Fig. 6 . Limited by the large E g and the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, pure g-C3N4 could not provide efficient photocatalytic performance under visible-light irradiation. However, the presence of CP-derived CDs in the CDCNs enabled them to sufficiently respond to visible light owing to the narrower bandgap formed by the CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 interface. A large number of photoinduced electron-hole pairs could be generated on the CB and VB of g-C3N4 after exposure to visible light (Eq. (1)). Simultaneously, the N atoms, which have strong electron affinity in CP-derived CDs, served as electron traps for electron capture and prevented the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby promoting reactive species generation (Eq. (2)). The separated photoexcited electrons on the composite photocatalyst could readily react with the chemisorbed O2 to generate O2
− (Eqs. (3) and (4)) in light of the more negative potential (-0.87 eV vs. NHE) for the E CB of CDCN-20% relative to the standard reduction potential of O2/O2
− (-0.33 eV vs. NHE) (Gordanshekan, Arabian, Nazar, Farhadian, & Tangestaninejad, 2023). Partial O2
− could further interact with h+ to produce 1O2 (Eq. (5)) (Lim et al., 2019). Moreover, the h+ on the VB of CDCN-20% could not directly oxidize OH− or H2O to yield
OH since the EVB value of CDCN-20% (+1.89 eV vs. NHE) was lower than the standard oxidation potential of OH−/
OH (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) and H2O/
OH (+2.68 eV vs. NHE) (Fattahimoghaddam, Mahvelati-Shamsabadi, & Lee, 2021). Interestingly, both the radical-trapping tests and EPR results indicated the presence of
OH in the CDCN-20% system under visible-light irradiation. Therefore, the production of
OH in the reaction might have occurred by the further reduction of O2
− (Eqs. (6) and (7)) (Khan, Han, Khan, Karim, & Cho, 2018). As stated above, O2
−, 1O2, and
OH were involved in the enhanced photodegradation of SY by CDCNs, in which O2
− played a major role (Eq. (8)).

In summary, carbon dots were successfully synthesized with citrus peel waste as the precursor by a green one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, novel metal-free CDCNs were designed via hydrothermal and physical self-assembly between CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 for SY photodecomposition under visible light. Notably, no additional passivators or additives were required during the entire nanocomposite fabrication process. The nanocomposites constructed by CP-derived CDs and g-C3N4 showed altered band structures and efficiently prevent the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance. Among all samples, CDCN-20% displayed the highest photocatalytic elimination efficiency for the food dye SY of 96.3% within 60 min. The radical-trapping tests and EPR spectra verified that O2
− was the predominant reactive species during the photocatalytic removal process of SY by CDCNs. Furthermore, the as-constructed CDCNs exhibited outstanding photostability, reusability and nontoxicity. This work not only reveals that high-quality CDCNs can be employed as an efficient photocatalyst for hazardous food colorant removal but also affords new avenues toward developing green and metal-free photocatalysts.
